refined_fr.refined_fr: The NGHE solution shall update the worksite model to reflect current equipment state, environmental conditions, and operational events with a p95 latency not exceeding the customer-defined target (baseline 1.0 s) across all supported update scenarios.
refined_fr.rationale: CR-NGHE-007 requires explicit binding to measurable latency acceptance criteria and clarification of what constitutes 'worksite model' (equipment state, environment, events). The refinement anchors the requirement to the p95 metric and customer-defined targets, enabling testable verification. High safety criticality demands that latency bounds protect operator situational awareness and safety decision-making.
refined_fr.verification_idea: Measure end-to-end latency from sensor/event generation through worksite model update completion across representative scenarios (equipment movement, obstacle detection, tele-operator command feedback, safety event propagation). Validate p95 latency against customer acceptance threshold in production network conditions.
sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Acquire real-time equipment state (position, orientation, operational mode) with sufficient frequency to support latency target.
FR-NGHE-007.2: Transmit state and event data from vehicle to worksite model service with bounded network latency.
FR-NGHE-007.3: Process and integrate incoming state/event data into worksite model representation with bounded computation latency.
FR-NGHE-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to all authorized consumers (operators, tele-operators, safety services) with bounded delivery latency.
FR-NGHE-007.5: Ensure worksite model update latency remains within bounds under network congestion, packet loss, and high-frequency update scenarios.

candidate_dps: DP-A: Centralized worksite model service with direct vehicle telemetry ingestion, synchronous processing, and broadcast distribution to all consumers.
DP-B: Distributed edge-local model caching on vehicle with asynchronous telemetry sync to central service; consumers query nearest cache or central service.
DP-C: Event-driven publish-subscribe architecture with latency-optimized message broker; vehicle publishes state/events, worksite model subscribes and updates, consumers subscribe to model updates.
DP-D: Hybrid hierarchical model with vehicle-local state cache, periodic batch sync to central service, and consumer-side interpolation for latency masking.
selected_dp.selected_dp: DP-C: Event-driven publish-subscribe architecture with latency-optimized message broker; vehicle publishes state/events, worksite model subscribes and updates, consumers subscribe to model updates.

potential_sources: Message broker performance and network topology coupling to latency target.
Consumer subscription filtering logic coupling to model update frequency.
Vehicle telemetry publishing rate coupling to worksite model update rate.
coupling_check.notes: DP-C decouples vehicle from direct model service dependency (reduces coupling vs. DP-A), enables asynchronous processing (supports latency bounds under load), and provides natural consumer subscription model. Coupling is primarily through message broker SLA and network link characteristics, which are external dependencies manageable via architecture and infrastructure design.

assumptions_risks: Assumption: Message broker (e.g., MQTT, Kafka, RabbitMQ) can be deployed with p95 latency < 100 ms for state/event delivery.
Assumption: Vehicle telemetry publishing frequency is sufficient (e.g., 10–50 Hz) to support 1.0 s p95 latency target.
Risk: Network congestion or packet loss may cause message broker latency to exceed target; mitigation requires QoS policies, priority queuing, and network provisioning.
Risk: Consumer subscription filtering or model update logic may introduce additional latency; mitigation requires performance profiling and optimization.
Risk: High-frequency updates may overwhelm consumers or storage; mitigation requires rate limiting, aggregation, or consumer-side filtering.
selected_dp.recommended_revision: Refine DP-C to explicitly specify: (1) message broker technology and SLA targets, (2) vehicle telemetry publishing frequency and payload size, (3) worksite model update processing logic (synchronous vs. asynchronous), (4) consumer subscription patterns and filtering rules, (5) network QoS and priority policies. These details are critical for latency verification and should be captured in lower-level DPs.

im_boundary.name: NGHE Solution
im_boundary.scope: Top-level system integration boundary encompassing vehicle telemetry acquisition, network transmission, worksite model service, and consumer distribution. Owns end-to-end latency accountability and orchestrates sub-module interactions.

external_interfaces.name: Telemetry
external_interfaces.details: Vehicle publishes real-time state (position, orientation, operational mode, sensor readings) and events (obstacle detection, mode changes, safety alerts) to worksite model service via message broker.

external_interfaces.name: Tasking
external_interfaces.details: Worksite model service distributes updated model state to operators and tele-operators for situational awareness and command planning.

external_interfaces.name: SafetyAlert
external_interfaces.details: Safety Services subscribes to worksite model updates and publishes safety alerts; NGHE Solution integrates alerts into model and distributes to operators.

external_interfaces.name: Network Link
external_interfaces.details: Underlying network infrastructure (WiFi, cellular, wired) providing connectivity between vehicle, worksite model service, and consumers; latency and reliability characteristics directly impact end-to-end latency.

module_containment_scores.notes: NGHE Solution is the top-level system boundary and naturally owns end-to-end worksite model update latency. All sub-components (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) contribute to this FR. Minimal diffusion; latency accountability is clearly scoped to NGHE Solution.

Vehicle owns telemetry acquisition and publishing (part of DP-C), but does not own worksite model service, consumer distribution, or network infrastructure. Assigning FR to Vehicle alone would diffuse latency accountability across multiple modules and obscure system-level latency targets.

Teleoperation is a consumer of worksite model updates, not the primary owner of model update latency. Assigning FR to Teleoperation would misalign ownership and obscure the system-level latency requirement.

Safety Services consumes worksite model updates and publishes safety alerts, but does not own the core model update latency. Assigning FR to Safety Services would diffuse accountability and create circular dependencies.

assigned_im.containment_hypothesis: NGHE Solution is the appropriate IM boundary because: (1) it owns the end-to-end latency accountability from telemetry acquisition through consumer distribution, (2) it integrates Vehicle, Teleoperation, and Safety Services sub-modules, (3) it provides a clear system-level interface for latency verification and acceptance testing, (4) it enables traceability from FR through DP to implementation modules (Vehicle telemetry, message broker, worksite model service, consumer distribution).
containment_risks: Risk: NGHE Solution is a high-level system boundary; detailed latency accountability must be decomposed to sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) in subsequent levels. Ensure sub-module latency budgets sum to system-level target.
Risk: Network Link is an external dependency (infrastructure) not fully owned by NGHE Solution; latency targets must account for network SLA and include contingency for network variability.

Risk: Consumer-side latency (e.g., Teleoperation UI rendering, Safety Services alert processing) may contribute to perceived latency; clarify whether FR-NGHE-007 includes consumer-side latency or only model update latency.
assigned_im.recommended_revision: Refine IM assignment to explicitly allocate latency budgets to sub-modules: (1) Vehicle: telemetry acquisition and publishing latency budget (e.g., 100 ms), (2) Network Link: transmission latency budget (e.g., 200 ms), (3) Worksite Model Service: ingestion and update processing latency budget (e.g., 300 ms), (4) Consumer Distribution: delivery latency budget (e.g., 400 ms). Total budget should not exceed p95 target (1.0 s) with margin for variability and contingency.

fr_dp_gate.reason: DP-C (event-driven publish-subscribe) directly addresses FR-NGHE-007 by enabling asynchronous, decoupled state/event propagation with bounded latency. The DP is logically sound, avoids unnecessary coupling, and is implementable with standard message broker technologies. No fundamental mismatch between requirement and design approach.

diffusion_risks: Risk: DP-C requires coordination across Vehicle (telemetry publisher), message broker (infrastructure), worksite model service (subscriber/updater), and consumers (subscribers). Latency accountability is distributed; ensure clear ownership and interface contracts.
Risk: Message broker is a critical infrastructure dependency; its performance directly impacts latency. Ensure message broker SLA is contractually bound and monitored.
Risk: Consumer-side latency (e.g., UI rendering, alert processing) may exceed model update latency; clarify scope of FR-NGHE-007 to avoid hidden diffusion.
dp_im_gate.containment_notes: NGHE Solution IM boundary appropriately contains the end-to-end latency accountability. Sub-modules (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) will be assigned specific latency budgets in decomposition. Network Link is an external dependency with clear interface (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link). Containment is sound; diffusion risks are manageable through clear interface contracts and latency budgets.

fr_im_gate.verification_feasibility: FR-NGHE-007 is verifiable through end-to-end latency measurement in production network conditions. Acceptance criteria (p95 ≤ 1.0 s) is measurable and testable. Verification requires instrumentation of telemetry acquisition, message broker, worksite model service, and consumer delivery to capture latency at each stage.
fr_im_gate.allocation_clarity: Allocation is clear: NGHE Solution owns end-to-end latency; sub-modules will be allocated specific latency budgets in decomposition. Interfaces (Telemetry, Tasking, SafetyAlert, Network Link) are well-defined and support latency measurement at module boundaries.

recommended_revisions: Clarify whether FR-NGHE-007 includes consumer-side latency (e.g., Teleoperation UI rendering, Safety Services alert processing) or only model update latency. This affects scope and verification approach.
Define latency measurement methodology: end-to-end (sensor to consumer display) vs. model update only (telemetry ingestion to model update completion). Specify measurement points and tools.
Establish latency budgets for sub-modules (Vehicle, Network, Worksite Model Service, Consumer Distribution) that sum to system-level target with margin for variability.
Specify message broker technology, QoS policies, and SLA targets to support latency verification and risk mitigation.

decision.justification: FR-NGHE-007 is not atomic and requires decomposition into sub-FRs (telemetry acquisition, network transmission, model processing, consumer distribution). DP-C (event-driven publish-subscribe) is sound and implementable, but latency accountability must be distributed across sub-modules with explicit budgets. NGHE Solution IM boundary is appropriate for system-level ownership, but sub-module assignments (Vehicle, Teleoperation, Safety Services) require detailed latency analysis in the next decomposition level. Proceed to decompose sub-FRs and allocate latency budgets.
next_level_sub_frs: FR-NGHE-007.1: Acquire real-time equipment state (position, orientation, operational mode) with sufficient frequency to support latency target.
FR-NGHE-007.2: Transmit state and event data from vehicle to worksite model service with bounded network latency.
FR-NGHE-007.3: Process and integrate incoming state/event data into worksite model representation with bounded computation latency.
FR-NGHE-007.4: Distribute updated worksite model to all authorized consumers (operators, tele-operators, safety services) with bounded delivery latency.
FR-NGHE-007.5: Ensure worksite model update latency remains within bounds under network congestion, packet loss, and high-frequency update scenarios.

assumptions: Message broker (e.g., MQTT, Kafka, RabbitMQ) can be deployed with p95 latency < 100 ms for state/event delivery.
Vehicle telemetry publishing frequency is sufficient (e.g., 10–50 Hz) to support 1.0 s p95 latency target.
Network infrastructure (WiFi, cellular, wired) can support required bandwidth and latency for worksite model updates.
Consumer-side latency (e.g., UI rendering, alert processing) is not included in FR-NGHE-007 scope; only model update latency is measured.
Worksite model update processing (ingestion, integration, distribution) can be completed within 300–400 ms budget.
open_risks: Network congestion or packet loss may cause message broker latency to exceed target; mitigation requires QoS policies, priority queuing, and network provisioning.
High-frequency updates may overwhelm consumers or storage; mitigation requires rate limiting, aggregation, or consumer-side filtering.
Message broker is a critical infrastructure dependency; its performance directly impacts latency. Ensure message broker SLA is contractually bound and monitored.
Consumer-side latency may exceed model update latency; clarify scope of FR-NGHE-007 to avoid hidden diffusion.
Safety criticality (high) requires robust latency guarantees; ensure latency verification includes worst-case scenarios and safety-critical update paths.
evidence_needed: Message broker technology selection and SLA documentation (latency, throughput, reliability).
Vehicle telemetry publishing frequency and payload size specifications.
Network infrastructure specifications (bandwidth, latency, reliability) and QoS policies.
Worksite model service architecture and processing latency estimates.
Consumer subscription patterns and filtering rules.
End-to-end latency measurement methodology and instrumentation plan.
Acceptance test plan with representative scenarios and p95 latency targets.
Safety analysis linking latency to operator situational awareness and safety decision-making.
